title.de: IAA Exam Scenario Flashcards — Lecture-Based Understanding
title.en: IAA Exam Scenario Flashcards — Lecture-Based Understanding

question.de: A dataset contains purchase amounts with values like 0, -999, and very large numbers. How should you approach this before training a model?
question.en: A dataset contains purchase amounts with values like 0, -999, and very large numbers. How should you approach this before training a model?

answer.de:
• Do not treat all numeric values as automatically valid
• Check whether 0 or -999 are real values, missing-value codes, or recording errors
• Use domain knowledge to decide which values are impossible or suspicious
• Visualize the distribution and inspect outliers
• Document whether values are corrected, removed, imputed, or kept as meaningful anomalies
answer.en:
• Do not treat all numeric values as automatically valid
• Check whether 0 or -999 are real values, missing-value codes, or recording errors
• Use domain knowledge to decide which values are impossible or suspicious
• Visualize the distribution and inspect outliers
• Document whether values are corrected, removed, imputed, or kept as meaningful anomalies

question.de: In a time series dataset, some sensor measurements are missing for short intervals. Why might estimation be preferred over deleting those rows?
question.en: In a time series dataset, some sensor measurements are missing for short intervals. Why might estimation be preferred over deleting those rows?

answer.de:
• Time series data has temporal dependency
• Deleting rows can break the regular sampling structure
• Nearby observations may contain useful information for estimating missing values
• Removing intervals may distort trend, seasonality, or sequential patterns
• The chosen estimation method should respect the time order and be documented
answer.en:
• Time series data has temporal dependency
• Deleting rows can break the regular sampling structure
• Nearby observations may contain useful information for estimating missing values
• Removing intervals may distort trend, seasonality, or sequential patterns
• The chosen estimation method should respect the time order and be documented

question.de: A model uses 200 predictors, but many are almost constant across all examples. What problem can this cause?
question.en: A model uses 200 predictors, but many are almost constant across all examples. What problem can this cause?

answer.de:
• Almost constant predictors usually contain little information
• They increase dimensionality without adding useful signal
• They can slow down training and make analysis less clear
• They may contribute noise or instability in some models
• Low-variance feature filtering can be considered before modeling
answer.en:
• Almost constant predictors usually contain little information
• They increase dimensionality without adding useful signal
• They can slow down training and make analysis less clear
• They may contribute noise or instability in some models
• Low-variance feature filtering can be considered before modeling

question.de: Two predictors are strongly correlated and both appear important. Why should you be careful when interpreting them?
question.en: Two predictors are strongly correlated and both appear important. Why should you be careful when interpreting them?

answer.de:
• They may carry almost the same information
• Their individual importance can be misleading
• Some models become unstable when predictors are highly correlated
• Removing or combining redundant predictors can simplify the model
• Interpretation should consider relationships among predictors, not only each predictor alone
answer.en:
• They may carry almost the same information
• Their individual importance can be misleading
• Some models become unstable when predictors are highly correlated
• Removing or combining redundant predictors can simplify the model
• Interpretation should consider relationships among predictors, not only each predictor alone

question.de: You run K-means several times and get different clusters each time. What does this tell you, and what should you do?
question.en: You run K-means several times and get different clusters each time. What does this tell you, and what should you do?

answer.de:
• K-means can depend strongly on centroid initialization
• Different runs may converge to different local solutions
• Run the algorithm multiple times with different initializations
• Compare cluster quality and stability
• Do not interpret one random run as definitive
answer.en:
• K-means can depend strongly on centroid initialization
• Different runs may converge to different local solutions
• Run the algorithm multiple times with different initializations
• Compare cluster quality and stability
• Do not interpret one random run as definitive

question.de: A domain expert says there should be three anomaly types, but the elbow method suggests four clusters. How should you decide?
question.en: A domain expert says there should be three anomaly types, but the elbow method suggests four clusters. How should you decide?

answer.de:
• Do not rely only on the elbow method
• Compare whether the four clusters have meaningful interpretation
• Ask whether the extra cluster reflects a real subtype, noise, or preprocessing artifact
• Use domain knowledge as an important decision criterion
• The chosen K should be useful for the task, not only numerically convenient
answer.en:
• Do not rely only on the elbow method
• Compare whether the four clusters have meaningful interpretation
• Ask whether the extra cluster reflects a real subtype, noise, or preprocessing artifact
• Use domain knowledge as an important decision criterion
• The chosen K should be useful for the task, not only numerically convenient

question.de: You reduce a dataset with PCA and the first two components explain much of the variance, but the plot does not separate the classes. What does this mean?
question.en: You reduce a dataset with PCA and the first two components explain much of the variance, but the plot does not separate the classes. What does this mean?

answer.de:
• PCA is unsupervised and does not use class labels
• High variance does not necessarily mean high class separability
• Class information may lie in lower-variance directions
• PCA can be useful for visualization but not guaranteed for classification
• Evaluate the final model performance instead of relying only on the PCA plot
answer.en:
• PCA is unsupervised and does not use class labels
• High variance does not necessarily mean high class separability
• Class information may lie in lower-variance directions
• PCA can be useful for visualization but not guaranteed for classification
• Evaluate the final model performance instead of relying only on the PCA plot

question.de: A housing-price model predicts poorly for very expensive houses, but reasonably well for average houses. What should you inspect?
question.en: A housing-price model predicts poorly for very expensive houses, but reasonably well for average houses. What should you inspect?

answer.de:
• Check whether expensive houses are underrepresented in training data
• Inspect residuals for systematic errors at high target values
• Check whether important luxury-related features are missing
• Consider whether the relationship is nonlinear
• Evaluate whether outliers are valid rare cases rather than errors
answer.en:
• Check whether expensive houses are underrepresented in training data
• Inspect residuals for systematic errors at high target values
• Check whether important luxury-related features are missing
• Consider whether the relationship is nonlinear
• Evaluate whether outliers are valid rare cases rather than errors

question.de: A student says the model is bad because training is slow. What should you check before changing the model type?
question.en: A student says the model is bad because training is slow. What should you check before changing the model type?

answer.de:
• Check whether the learning rate is too small
• Check whether features have very different scales
• Inspect whether the cost function is decreasing steadily
• Consider mini-batch or stochastic learning for large datasets
• Slow optimization does not automatically mean the model class is inappropriate
answer.en:
• Check whether the learning rate is too small
• Check whether features have very different scales
• Inspect whether the cost function is decreasing steadily
• Consider mini-batch or stochastic learning for large datasets
• Slow optimization does not automatically mean the model class is inappropriate

question.de: A logistic regression model gives many probabilities close to 0.5. What does this suggest about the classification problem?
question.en: A logistic regression model gives many probabilities close to 0.5. What does this suggest about the classification problem?

answer.de:
• The model is uncertain for many examples
• The classes may overlap in the current feature space
• Important features or nonlinear transformations may be missing
• The decision boundary may not fit the true structure well
• Inspect data representation, feature quality, and model complexity
answer.en:
• The model is uncertain for many examples
• The classes may overlap in the current feature space
• Important features or nonlinear transformations may be missing
• The decision boundary may not fit the true structure well
• Inspect data representation, feature quality, and model complexity

question.de: A model for fraud detection has high accuracy but almost never predicts fraud. What is the main issue?
question.en: A model for fraud detection has high accuracy but almost never predicts fraud. What is the main issue?

answer.de:
• The dataset is likely imbalanced
• Accuracy is dominated by the majority non-fraud class
• The model may be useless for the minority class
• Inspect recall, precision, F1-score, and the confusion matrix
• Consider resampling, class weighting, threshold adjustment, or anomaly-oriented methods
answer.en:
• The dataset is likely imbalanced
• Accuracy is dominated by the majority non-fraud class
• The model may be useless for the minority class
• Inspect recall, precision, F1-score, and the confusion matrix
• Consider resampling, class weighting, threshold adjustment, or anomaly-oriented methods

question.de: A Naive Bayes spam classifier sees a word in the test email that never appeared in spam emails during training. What can go wrong?
question.en: A Naive Bayes spam classifier sees a word in the test email that never appeared in spam emails during training. What can go wrong?

answer.de:
• The model may assign zero probability to that word for the spam class
• A zero probability can dominate the class probability calculation
• This is the zero-frequency problem
• The classifier may reject a class too strongly because of limited training data
• Smoothing is commonly used to reduce this issue
answer.en:
• The model may assign zero probability to that word for the spam class
• A zero probability can dominate the class probability calculation
• This is the zero-frequency problem
• The classifier may reject a class too strongly because of limited training data
• Smoothing is commonly used to reduce this issue

question.de: A decision tree is very easy to explain to managers but performs worse than a Random Forest. How should you discuss the trade-off?
question.en: A decision tree is very easy to explain to managers but performs worse than a Random Forest. How should you discuss the trade-off?

answer.de:
• A single tree is more interpretable
• A Random Forest often generalizes better because it reduces variance
• The best choice depends on whether interpretability or predictive performance is more important
• For high-stakes decisions, interpretability may be essential
• For prediction-focused tasks, an ensemble may be justified if properly evaluated
answer.en:
• A single tree is more interpretable
• A Random Forest often generalizes better because it reduces variance
• The best choice depends on whether interpretability or predictive performance is more important
• For high-stakes decisions, interpretability may be essential
• For prediction-focused tasks, an ensemble may be justified if properly evaluated

question.de: A linear SVM performs poorly on a dataset where classes form circular regions. What should you consider?
question.en: A linear SVM performs poorly on a dataset where classes form circular regions. What should you consider?

answer.de:
• The classes are probably not linearly separable in the original feature space
• A nonlinear kernel such as RBF may be more appropriate
• Feature transformations could also help
• Hyperparameters should be tuned using validation data
• Feature scaling is important before using distance-based kernels
answer.en:
• The classes are probably not linearly separable in the original feature space
• A nonlinear kernel such as RBF may be more appropriate
• Feature transformations could also help
• Hyperparameters should be tuned using validation data
• Feature scaling is important before using distance-based kernels

question.de: A k-NN classifier performs badly after adding many irrelevant features. Why can this happen?
question.en: A k-NN classifier performs badly after adding many irrelevant features. Why can this happen?

answer.de:
• k-NN depends directly on distances between examples
• Irrelevant features can distort distances
• Truly similar examples may appear far apart
• This is especially problematic in high-dimensional spaces
• Feature selection and scaling are important preprocessing steps for k-NN
answer.en:
• k-NN depends directly on distances between examples
• Irrelevant features can distort distances
• Truly similar examples may appear far apart
• This is especially problematic in high-dimensional spaces
• Feature selection and scaling are important preprocessing steps for k-NN

question.de: A classifier for cancer screening has many false positives but very few false negatives. Is this necessarily bad?
question.en: A classifier for cancer screening has many false positives but very few false negatives. Is this necessarily bad?

answer.de:
• Not necessarily
• In screening, missing a sick patient can be much worse than sending a healthy patient for further testing
• High recall may be more important than high precision
• The acceptability depends on follow-up cost, patient risk, and clinical context
• The confusion matrix must be interpreted with domain consequences
answer.en:
• Not necessarily
• In screening, missing a sick patient can be much worse than sending a healthy patient for further testing
• High recall may be more important than high precision
• The acceptability depends on follow-up cost, patient risk, and clinical context
• The confusion matrix must be interpreted with domain consequences

question.de: You oversample the minority class by duplicating the same rare examples many times. What risk does this create?
question.en: You oversample the minority class by duplicating the same rare examples many times. What risk does this create?

answer.de:
• The model may memorize duplicated examples
• Minority-class overfitting can increase
• Validation performance may look better than real generalization
• More diverse synthetic examples or careful resampling may be preferable
• The final test set should reflect the real data distribution
answer.en:
• The model may memorize duplicated examples
• Minority-class overfitting can increase
• Validation performance may look better than real generalization
• More diverse synthetic examples or careful resampling may be preferable
• The final test set should reflect the real data distribution

question.de: A student chooses the best hyperparameters using the test set because it gives the most reliable estimate. What is wrong with this reasoning?
question.en: A student chooses the best hyperparameters using the test set because it gives the most reliable estimate. What is wrong with this reasoning?

answer.de:
• The test set must remain untouched until final evaluation
• Using it for hyperparameter selection leaks information into model choice
• The final test performance becomes overly optimistic
• Hyperparameters should be selected using validation data or cross-validation
• The test set answers only the final question: how well does the selected model generalize?
answer.en:
• The test set must remain untouched until final evaluation
• Using it for hyperparameter selection leaks information into model choice
• The final test performance becomes overly optimistic
• Hyperparameters should be selected using validation data or cross-validation
• The test set answers only the final question: how well does the selected model generalize?

question.de: A model has high training error and high validation error. Your colleague suggests collecting more data. Is that the first fix?
question.en: A model has high training error and high validation error. Your colleague suggests collecting more data. Is that the first fix?

answer.de:
• Probably not the first fix
• High training and validation error suggest high bias or underfitting
• More data usually does not help much if the model is too simple
• Try more relevant features, greater model complexity, or less regularization
• Learning curves can confirm whether more data is likely to help
answer.en:
• Probably not the first fix
• High training and validation error suggest high bias or underfitting
• More data usually does not help much if the model is too simple
• Try more relevant features, greater model complexity, or less regularization
• Learning curves can confirm whether more data is likely to help

question.de: A model has very low training error but much higher validation error. What actions are reasonable?
question.en: A model has very low training error but much higher validation error. What actions are reasonable?

answer.de:
• The model likely has high variance or overfitting
• Collecting more representative training data may help
• Stronger regularization can help
• Reducing model complexity or noisy features can help
• Bagging methods may also reduce variance
answer.en:
• The model likely has high variance or overfitting
• Collecting more representative training data may help
• Stronger regularization can help
• Reducing model complexity or noisy features can help
• Bagging methods may also reduce variance

question.de: You train many deep decision trees on bootstrap samples and average their predictions. What problem are you mainly trying to reduce?
question.en: You train many deep decision trees on bootstrap samples and average their predictions. What problem are you mainly trying to reduce?

answer.de:
• You are mainly trying to reduce variance
• Deep trees are flexible and unstable
• Bootstrap sampling creates different versions of the training data
• Averaging or voting reduces dependence on one particular tree
• This is the core idea behind bagging-style ensembles
answer.en:
• You are mainly trying to reduce variance
• Deep trees are flexible and unstable
• Bootstrap sampling creates different versions of the training data
• Averaging or voting reduces dependence on one particular tree
• This is the core idea behind bagging-style ensembles

question.de: A boosting model performs very well on training data but poorly on validation data, especially with noisy labels. Why?
question.en: A boosting model performs very well on training data but poorly on validation data, especially with noisy labels. Why?

answer.de:
• Boosting focuses more on examples that previous models misclassified
• Noisy or wrongly labeled examples may receive too much attention
• The model can start fitting label noise
• This increases overfitting risk
• Better label quality, regularization, or fewer boosting iterations may be needed
answer.en:
• Boosting focuses more on examples that previous models misclassified
• Noisy or wrongly labeled examples may receive too much attention
• The model can start fitting label noise
• This increases overfitting risk
• Better label quality, regularization, or fewer boosting iterations may be needed

question.de: In stacking, why should the meta-classifier not be trained on predictions from base models evaluated on their own training data?
question.en: In stacking, why should the meta-classifier not be trained on predictions from base models evaluated on their own training data?

answer.de:
• The base-model predictions would be overly optimistic
• The meta-classifier would learn from leaked training performance
• It may overestimate how reliable each base model is
• Out-of-fold predictions are safer for training the meta-classifier
• This prevents the stacking system from learning unrealistic patterns
answer.en:
• The base-model predictions would be overly optimistic
• The meta-classifier would learn from leaked training performance
• It may overestimate how reliable each base model is
• Out-of-fold predictions are safer for training the meta-classifier
• This prevents the stacking system from learning unrealistic patterns

question.de: A speech-recognition model fails mostly when there is car noise in the background. What would a data-centric response be?
question.en: A speech-recognition model fails mostly when there is car noise in the background. What would a data-centric response be?

answer.de:
• Collect or improve examples with car-noise background
• Check whether labels are consistent for noisy speech
• Ensure that this condition is sufficiently represented in training and validation data
• Improve the dataset rather than only tuning the architecture
• Use error analysis to guide targeted data improvement
answer.en:
• Collect or improve examples with car-noise background
• Check whether labels are consistent for noisy speech
• Ensure that this condition is sufficiently represented in training and validation data
• Improve the dataset rather than only tuning the architecture
• Use error analysis to guide targeted data improvement

question.de: A customer-churn model worked well last year, but customer behavior changed after a new competitor entered the market. What type of problem may this be?
question.en: A customer-churn model worked well last year, but customer behavior changed after a new competitor entered the market. What type of problem may this be?

answer.de:
• This may be concept drift
• The relationship between input features and churn may have changed
• The old patterns learned by the model may no longer hold
• Monitoring should detect performance degradation
• Retraining or redesigning features may be necessary
answer.en:
• This may be concept drift
• The relationship between input features and churn may have changed
• The old patterns learned by the model may no longer hold
• Monitoring should detect performance degradation
• Retraining or redesigning features may be necessary

question.de: A shallow neural network performs poorly on image data. Why might simply adding more raw pixels not solve the problem?
question.en: A shallow neural network performs poorly on image data. Why might simply adding more raw pixels not solve the problem?

answer.de:
• Raw images are high-dimensional
• More pixels increase the number of parameters and overfitting risk
• A shallow network may not learn hierarchical visual features effectively
• Image structure such as locality is not explicitly exploited
• CNNs are often more appropriate for image data
answer.en:
• Raw images are high-dimensional
• More pixels increase the number of parameters and overfitting risk
• A shallow network may not learn hierarchical visual features effectively
• Image structure such as locality is not explicitly exploited
• CNNs are often more appropriate for image data

question.de: During neural network training, all hidden neurons in a layer learn the same thing. What initialization mistake may have caused this?
question.en: During neural network training, all hidden neurons in a layer learn the same thing. What initialization mistake may have caused this?

answer.de:
• The weights may have been initialized identically, for example all zeros
• Identical neurons receive identical gradients
• They update in the same way and fail to specialize
• Random initialization breaks symmetry
• Proper initialization helps different neurons learn different features
answer.en:
• The weights may have been initialized identically, for example all zeros
• Identical neurons receive identical gradients
• They update in the same way and fail to specialize
• Random initialization breaks symmetry
• Proper initialization helps different neurons learn different features

question.de: A network with ReLU units stops improving, and many neurons output zero for all examples. What problem may have occurred?
question.en: A network with ReLU units stops improving, and many neurons output zero for all examples. What problem may have occurred?

answer.de:
• This may be the dying ReLU problem
• ReLU has zero gradient for negative inputs
• Some neurons may stop receiving useful updates
• A smaller learning rate or better initialization may help
• Leaky ReLU can reduce this risk by allowing a small negative-side signal
answer.en:
• This may be the dying ReLU problem
• ReLU has zero gradient for negative inputs
• Some neurons may stop receiving useful updates
• A smaller learning rate or better initialization may help
• Leaky ReLU can reduce this risk by allowing a small negative-side signal

question.de: Why is a CNN usually better suited than a fully connected network for classifying images?
question.en: Why is a CNN usually better suited than a fully connected network for classifying images?

answer.de:
• CNNs exploit local spatial structure in images
• Filters are shared across locations
• This greatly reduces the number of parameters
• CNNs can detect the same feature in different positions
• They learn hierarchical visual features from edges to object parts
answer.en:
• CNNs exploit local spatial structure in images
• Filters are shared across locations
• This greatly reduces the number of parameters
• CNNs can detect the same feature in different positions
• They learn hierarchical visual features from edges to object parts

question.de: A CNN detects edges in early layers and object parts in deeper layers. Why is this expected?
question.en: A CNN detects edges in early layers and object parts in deeper layers. Why is this expected?

answer.de:
• Early layers see small local regions
• They learn simple visual patterns such as edges, colors, and textures
• Deeper layers combine earlier features into more complex structures
• Later layers can respond to object parts or semantic patterns
• This is hierarchical feature learning
answer.en:
• Early layers see small local regions
• They learn simple visual patterns such as edges, colors, and textures
• Deeper layers combine earlier features into more complex structures
• Later layers can respond to object parts or semantic patterns
• This is hierarchical feature learning

question.de: You have only 300 labeled medical images. Why might training a CNN from scratch be a poor idea?
question.en: You have only 300 labeled medical images. Why might training a CNN from scratch be a poor idea?

answer.de:
• CNNs usually need large datasets
• Training from scratch may overfit quickly
• Computational cost can be high
• Transfer learning can reuse features from a pretrained model
• However, domain mismatch must be checked because medical images may differ strongly from natural images
answer.en:
• CNNs usually need large datasets
• Training from scratch may overfit quickly
• Computational cost can be high
• Transfer learning can reuse features from a pretrained model
• However, domain mismatch must be checked because medical images may differ strongly from natural images

question.de: You augment digit images by rotating them strongly, but performance drops. What might be wrong?
question.en: You augment digit images by rotating them strongly, but performance drops. What might be wrong?

answer.de:
• The augmentation may change the label meaning
• A strongly rotated digit can become ambiguous or resemble another class
• Augmentation must preserve the true class
• More data is not useful if it introduces wrong or unrealistic examples
• Choose transformations that match expected real-world variation
answer.en:
• The augmentation may change the label meaning
• A strongly rotated digit can become ambiguous or resemble another class
• Augmentation must preserve the true class
• More data is not useful if it introduces wrong or unrealistic examples
• Choose transformations that match expected real-world variation

question.de: A standard feedforward neural network is used for sentences by treating each word position as a separate feature. What problems can occur?
question.en: A standard feedforward neural network is used for sentences by treating each word position as a separate feature. What problems can occur?

answer.de:
• Sentences can have different lengths
• The model does not naturally share features across positions
• A word learned at one position may not generalize to another position
• Input dimensionality can become very large
• Sequence models are designed to handle ordered dependencies more naturally
answer.en:
• Sentences can have different lengths
• The model does not naturally share features across positions
• A word learned at one position may not generalize to another position
• Input dimensionality can become very large
• Sequence models are designed to handle ordered dependencies more naturally

question.de: An RNN correctly handles short phrases but fails when important words are far apart. What is the likely limitation?
question.en: An RNN correctly handles short phrases but fails when important words are far apart. What is the likely limitation?

answer.de:
• Standard RNNs struggle with long-range dependencies
• Gradients can vanish through many time steps
• Earlier information may be forgotten
• LSTM or GRU units can help preserve information longer
• The model architecture should match the dependency length of the task
answer.en:
• Standard RNNs struggle with long-range dependencies
• Gradients can vanish through many time steps
• Earlier information may be forgotten
• LSTM or GRU units can help preserve information longer
• The model architecture should match the dependency length of the task

question.de: You forecast electricity demand with random train-test splitting and get excellent results. Why should you be suspicious?
question.en: You forecast electricity demand with random train-test splitting and get excellent results. Why should you be suspicious?

answer.de:
• Random splitting can mix future observations into training
• Time series validation must respect chronological order
• The model may indirectly learn from future patterns
• Performance can become unrealistically optimistic
• Train on past data and test on later periods
answer.en:
• Random splitting can mix future observations into training
• Time series validation must respect chronological order
• The model may indirectly learn from future patterns
• Performance can become unrealistically optimistic
• Train on past data and test on later periods

question.de: After removing trend and seasonality, the residuals still show a clear repeating pattern. What does this imply?
question.en: After removing trend and seasonality, the residuals still show a clear repeating pattern. What does this imply?

answer.de:
• The model did not capture all systematic structure
• The decomposition or forecasting model may be incomplete
• Another seasonal component or external factor may exist
• Residuals should ideally look random and uncorrelated
• Patterned residuals indicate room for model improvement
answer.en:
• The model did not capture all systematic structure
• The decomposition or forecasting model may be incomplete
• Another seasonal component or external factor may exist
• Residuals should ideally look random and uncorrelated
• Patterned residuals indicate room for model improvement

question.de: For a small sequence dataset, why might a GRU be preferred over an LSTM?
question.en: For a small sequence dataset, why might a GRU be preferred over an LSTM?

answer.de:
• GRUs have a simpler architecture
• They usually have fewer parameters
• They can train faster and may overfit less on small datasets
• Their performance is often comparable for many sequence tasks
• LSTMs may be preferred when very long-term memory control is needed
answer.en:
• GRUs have a simpler architecture
• They usually have fewer parameters
• They can train faster and may overfit less on small datasets
• Their performance is often comparable for many sequence tasks
• LSTMs may be preferred when very long-term memory control is needed

question.de: A manufacturing sensor suddenly reports values far outside the normal range. What questions should be asked before calling it a real anomaly?
question.en: A manufacturing sensor suddenly reports values far outside the normal range. What questions should be asked before calling it a real anomaly?

answer.de:
• Could it be a sensor malfunction or pipeline error?
• Is the value physically possible?
• Did operating conditions change?
• Do other related sensors confirm the event?
• Anomaly detection should be followed by anomaly analysis and interpretation
answer.en:
• Could it be a sensor malfunction or pipeline error?
• Is the value physically possible?
• Did operating conditions change?
• Do other related sensors confirm the event?
• Anomaly detection should be followed by anomaly analysis and interpretation

question.de: A single login at 3 a.m. is normal for night-shift workers but suspicious for office employees. What anomaly type does this illustrate?
question.en: A single login at 3 a.m. is normal for night-shift workers but suspicious for office employees. What anomaly type does this illustrate?

answer.de:
• This illustrates a contextual anomaly
• The same data point can be normal or abnormal depending on context
• Relevant context may include time, role, location, or user behavior
• A context-free detector may produce misleading alerts
• Anomaly detection must consider domain-specific normal behavior
answer.en:
• This illustrates a contextual anomaly
• The same data point can be normal or abnormal depending on context
• Relevant context may include time, role, location, or user behavior
• A context-free detector may produce misleading alerts
• Anomaly detection must consider domain-specific normal behavior

question.de: A loan model has high overall accuracy but rejects one demographic group much more often. What should be investigated?
question.en: A loan model has high overall accuracy but rejects one demographic group much more often. What should be investigated?

answer.de:
• Check representativeness of the training data
• Compare error rates across demographic groups
• Investigate whether historical bias is encoded in labels or features
• Check whether the optimization metric hides unequal harm
• Responsible AI requires fairness, transparency, and accountability analysis
answer.en:
• Check representativeness of the training data
• Compare error rates across demographic groups
• Investigate whether historical bias is encoded in labels or features
• Check whether the optimization metric hides unequal harm
• Responsible AI requires fairness, transparency, and accountability analysis
